executiveSummary: NordMach should launch PulseGuard on a segmented site subscription: a flat monthly fee per connected site, set separately for enterprise and mid-market plants, with a bounded outcome-linked amendment held as an enterprise opt-in for renewal conversations. The pilot demonstrated ~35% lower unplanned downtime at participating accounts [EV-04], worth ~£1,800 per avoided hour at enterprise sites [EV-08]. The modelled base case reaches cumulative contribution break-even in month {{breakeven-months}}, with year-1 service revenue of {{revenue-y1}} rising to {{revenue-y3}} by year 3. The recommendation is conditional: willingness-to-pay research and a second mid-market customer voice are required before price levels are locked for the board.
situation: NordMach has completed an 18-month PulseGuard pilot and must choose a commercial pricing model for launch through its direct channel [EV-04]. The board requires a decision next quarter, and Finance requires contribution-positive economics within 18 months of launch [EV-10]. The installed base spans two segments with different buying behaviour: ~420 machines across 12 enterprise accounts and ~880 machines across 110 mid-market plants [EV-02]. Near-term cash is constrained following the Doncaster investment [EV-03].
diagnosis: The evidence separates the segments cleanly. Enterprise customers measure downtime (~£1,800/hour) [EV-08], accept availability KPIs, and would accept a capped, floored, jointly measured outcome fee [EV-18] — but resist per-machine fees on idle backup machines and prefer site-level agreements [EV-19]. Mid-market plants do not measure downtime cost [EV-09], reject performance-linked billing outright [EV-20], and want a fixed plant-level fee they can budget [EV-21]. This directly contradicts the internal belief that all customers will accept outcome pricing [EV-11]; the contradiction is preserved, not resolved — outcome acceptance is segment-specific at best. Competitively, SentinelIQ anchors software-only monitoring at £49/machine/month list [EV-22], while MacroFleet bundles at ~4% of equipment price on new sales only, leaving installed-base retrofit — NordMach's main opportunity — unaddressed [EV-23]. The only attach-rate benchmark is from 2018 and is treated as a stale lower bound [EV-24].
optionsNarrative: Four models were considered. (1) Bundling into equipment price monetises new sales only, buries service margin [EV-15] and removes the sales commission line [EV-12] — rejected. (2) Per-connected-machine pricing is administratively easy [EV-17] and matches the SentinelIQ anchor [EV-22], but enterprise customers refuse to pay for idle backup machines [EV-19] — rejected as the lead model. (3) A segmented site subscription matches how both segments buy [EV-19, EV-21], keeps the price sheet simple for the sales team [EV-13], and models to break-even in month {{breakeven-months}} in the base case — recommended. (4) An enterprise outcome-linked amendment (reduced base plus capped share of jointly measured avoided downtime) is supported by enterprise evidence [EV-18] and Finance's cap/floor requirement [EV-14], but requires per-site measurement projects [EV-17] — retained as an opt-in branch, piloted at renewal with the pilot accounts.
recommendationNarrative: Launch the segmented site subscription at the modelled levels, phased to service capacity of one engineer per ~200 connected machines [EV-16]. Year-1 contribution is modelled at {{contribution-y1}}, turning positive with cumulative break-even in month {{breakeven-months}} and cumulative contribution of {{cumulative-y3}} by end of year 3. Hold the enterprise outcome amendment for renewal conversations at the three pilot accounts, subject to agreed measurement protocols, caps and floors [EV-14, EV-18]. Conditions: complete willingness-to-pay validation and a second mid-market interview before the board fixes price levels; confirm phased-onboarding hiring plan with Operations.
risksNarrative: Material risks: pilot performance may not generalise beyond hand-picked sites [EV-07]; mid-market value evidence is an unmeasured estimate [EV-09] and the segment was never piloted; adoption below the modelled switching value would breach the 18-month constraint [EV-10]; and the CEO's 20%-of-value willingness-to-pay claim remains an unsupported assertion that must not be presented as evidence [EV-01]. A quarantined document containing a rival's non-public discount schedule exists and has been excluded from this work pending specialist competition review; no price level herein derives from it.
